title: Sampling method considerations
description: Identify the advantages and disadvantages of sampling methods, why we might use one sampling method over another, and how to set up a sample so it represents the population.

assessment_items.question_content:
A concert venue offers tickets in $3$ zones: orchestra, grand tier, and balcony. Tickets in the orchestra zone are most expensive, and tickets in the balcony zone are least expensive. Managers of the venue want to survey approximately $150$ guests at the next concert about their opinions on the schedule of shows in the upcoming season. 

**Why might the managers choose a systematic random sample instead of a simple random sample?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A systematic sample can save time by selecting guests as they enter rather than looking for guests selected in a simple random sample.
A systematic random sample reduces the likelihood of getting disproportionate numbers of guests from any given zone.
A systematic sample chooses some guests of all ages, so they will be more likely to get a representative sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
At Amira's pizza shop, they open a fresh batch of pizza sauce for every $10^\text{th}$ pizza they make. Amira decides she wants to take a sample of pizzas in a given day and evaluate their overall quality. Her shop makes approximately $150$ pizzas per day, and she doesn't want to sample more than $10$ pizzas for time's sake.

Amira decides to use a systematic random sample where she'll evaluate every $k^\text{th}$ pizza made that day.

**Why might Amira choose $k=17$ instead of $k=10$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Amira will sample more than $10$ pizzas if she uses $k=10$.
Larger values of $k$ produce samples that are generally more representative of the population.
In terms of pizza sauce freshness, Amira will likely get a more representative sample if she uses $k=17$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large bakery produces cakes in an assembly line. Sergio noticed a potential defect that seems to be showing in every other cake. He decides that in the next production run, he'll take a random sample of cakes to get a better idea of how many cakes might have this defect.

**Why might Sergio choose a simple random sample instead of a systematic random sample?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A simple random sample can save time since it is less involved than a systematic random sample.
If the defect is in every other cake, a systematic random sample could miss it completely depending on what Sergio chooses for $k$.
A simple random sample guarantees that Sergio will get some cakes from each hour of production.

assessment_items.question_content:
A concert venue offers tickets in $3$ zones: orchestra, grand tier, and balcony. Tickets in the orchestra zone are most expensive, and tickets in the balcony zone are least expensive. Managers of the venue want to survey approximately $150$ guests at an upcoming concert about their overall experience. They want to take a systematic random sample of the $3000$ guests they expect to attend.

**Which of these strategies will accomplish their intended design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Invite the first $150$ guests that arrive to participate in the survey.
Randomly select one of the first $20$ guests to arrive and every $20^\text{th}$ guest thereafter to take the survey.
Use a computer to randomly select $150$ tickets and invite those guests to participate in the survey.
Randomly select a row, and invite every guest in that row to participate in the survey. Continue to randomly select rows like this until about $150$ total guests have been selected.
Randomly select $50$ tickets from each zone and invite those guests to participate in the survey.

assessment_items.question_content:
A college professor wants to survey a sample of students taking her large lecture course. There are about $150$ students in the course, and $10$ of those students are graduate students. She wants to take a systematic random sample of approximately $30$ students. 

**Which of these strategies will accomplish her intended design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Randomly select $15$ men and $15$ women from the class for the survey.
Randomly select $2$ graduate students and $28$ other students for the survey.
Randomly select one of the first $5$ students to arrive to class, and every $5^\text{th}$ student thereafter to take the survey.
Randomly select one of the first $15$ students to arrive to class, and every $15^\text{th}$ student thereafter to take the survey.
Assign each student a number and use a computer to randomly select $30$ students for the survey.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jerome's favorite *Harry Potter* book has $22$ chapters, $500$ pages, and approximately $100{,}000$ words. He's curious how many of the words are "made up" words or names that don't exist in the dictionary.

He wants to take a systematic random sample of about $500$ words to estimate what percent of the words in the book are made up.

**Which of these strategies will accomplish his intended design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Randomly select one of the first $20$ words and every $20^\text{th}$ word thereafter for the sample.
Randomly select one of the first $200$ words and every $200^\text{th}$ word thereafter for the sample.
Randomly select $23$ words from each chapter.
Randomly select $1$ word from each page.
Assign every word a number and use a computer to randomly select $500$ numbers with no repeats.

assessment_items.question_content:
Forest rangers at a national park want to take a sample of trees to estimate what proportion of trees in the park are infected with a certain disease. The population of trees in question is divided by a creek. East of the creek, most of the trees are oak. West of the creek, most trees are cedar, which are more likely to be infected.

The rangers are considering taking a stratified random sample using each side of the creek as strata. They'll sample trees from each side proportionately based on the total number of trees on each side. 

**Why might the rangers choose stratification instead of a simple random sample to estimate the proportion of infected trees?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: In repeated sampling, estimates from this sort of stratified sample would likely vary less than estimates from simple random samples.
A stratified sample eliminates the bias that arises from using a simple random sample.
A stratified random sample reduces the likelihood of getting disproportionate numbers of cedar or oak trees in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
An airline offers a certain flight once per day that usually contains about $250$ passengers. The flight offers seats in first class (most expensive), business class, and economy class (least expensive). The airline wants to survey $500$ passengers of this flight about their overall satisfaction. The passengers will be selected using a cluster random sample where each flight is a cluster.

**Why might the airline choose a cluster random sample instead of a simple random sample in this setting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Contacting all of the passengers on a few flights will probably be more efficient than contacting passengers spread out across many flights.
A simple random sample might result in a disproportionate number of passengers from one or more class, but a cluster sample is less likely to suffer this issue.
A cluster sample chooses some passengers from each flight, so the airline will be more likely to get a representative sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A warehouse stores thousands of marble slabs of different shapes, sizes, and materials that are used for construction projects. The manager wants to estimate the average weight of these slabs. The slabs are arranged in stacks that have about $10$ slabs of different varieties. The manager wants to weigh a random sample of $100$ slabs.

**Why might the manager choose a cluster random sample instead of a stratified random sample in this setting?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It will probably be easier to weigh heavy slabs that are stacked together rather than pulling out some slabs from different stacks.
Each stack has a variety of slabs, so choosing all of the slabs in some of the stacks should represent the population fairly well.
A cluster sample chooses some slabs from each stack, so the manager will be more likely to get a representative sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A concert venue offers tickets in $3$ zones: orchestra, grand tier, and balcony. Tickets in the orchestra zone are most expensive, and tickets in the balcony zone are least expensive. Managers of the venue want to survey approximately $150$ guests at an upcoming concert about their overall experience. They want to take a stratified random sample of guests based on the venue's zones.

**Which of these strategies will accomplish their intended design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Invite the first $150$ guests that arrive to participate in the survey.
Invite every $10^{\text{th}}$ guest who arrives to participate in the survey until $150$ people have been selected.
Use a computer to randomly select $150$ tickets and invite those guests to participate in the survey.
Randomly select a row, and invite every guest in that row to participate in the survey. Continue to randomly select rows like this until about $150$ total guests have been selected.
Randomly select $50$ tickets from each zone and invite those guests to participate in the survey.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lilian's favorite magazine published $50$ issues last year, and each issue contained approximately $250$ pages. She wants to take a cluster random sample of about $1{,}000$ total pages to estimate what proportion of all pages contained an advertisement. 

**Which of these strategies will accomplish her intended design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Examine the first $50$ pages in the $20$ most recent issues.
Put the issues in a random order and examine every $10^{\text{th}}$ page until $1{,}000$ pages have been examined.
Assign a unique number to each page, and use a computer to randomly select $1{,}000$ of those numbers.
Randomly select $4$ issues, and examine every page in those issues.
Examine $20$ randomly selected pages from each of the $50$ issues.

assessment_items.question_content:
A college professor wants to survey a sample of students taking his course. Here are some details about his course:

* He teaches $5$ sections of the course.
* There are $250$ total students across those sections.
* There are $50$ graduate students and $200$ undergraduate students taking the course.
* Each section has about $50$ students (some graduate and some undergraduate).

The professor wants to take a sample of $30$ students for a survey. He suspects that opinions on the survey may differ the most based on student type (graduate or undergraduate), so he wants to design his sample to take that into account.

**Which of these strategies will accomplish his intended design?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Randomly select $6$ students from each section for the survey.
Randomly select $6$ graduate students and $24$ undergraduate students for the survey.
For each section, randomly select one of the first $8$ students to arrive to class, and every $8^\text{th}$ student thereafter to take the survey.
For each section, randomly select one of the first $12$ students to arrive to class, and every $12^\text{th}$ student thereafter to take the survey.
Randomly select one of the sections, and give the survey to every student in that section.
